title: Memories of Childhood by Zitkala-Sa and Bama
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Racial discrimination, caste discrimination, cultural suppression, humiliation, resistance, and the power of education.

summary:
This chapter has two autobiographical excerpts about childhood experiences with discrimination.

Part 1, “The Cutting of My Long Hair” by Zitkala-Sa, describes the experience of a Native American girl from the Dakota tribe. She is sent to a boarding school run by white people where she faces cultural oppression. On her first day, she is treated like an animal. She is stared at, her blanket is taken, and she is forced to wear tight shoes. At breakfast, she does not know the rules and is scolded for eating without prayer.

The worst experience comes when she hears that the school plans to cut the girls’ long hair. In her culture, only cowards or mourners have short hair, while warriors wear long hair. She hides under a bed to resist, but she is dragged out, tied to a chair, and her thick braids are cut off. She feels humiliated and loses her spirit. She compares the experience to being an animal caught by hunters. The incident marks her loss of identity and dignity and shows how the school wants to erase her culture.

Part 2, “We Too Are Human Beings” by Bama, is about caste discrimination. Bama, a Tamil Dalit writer, recalls an incident from her childhood. While walking home from school, she sees an elder from her community carrying a packet of vadai by its string and holding it away from his body. He gives it to the landlord without touching him directly. Bama finds this funny and laughs.

Later, her elder brother Annan explains that the man was from a low caste and could not touch the food meant for an upper-caste landlord. This was untouchability. Bama is shocked and angry. The incident awakens her to the social injustice around her. Annan tells her that education is the only way to earn respect and fight the system. Inspired, Bama studies hard and stands first in her class. Many friends come to her, proving her brother right.

Both women face humiliation because of their birth, one for being Native American and the other for being Dalit. Both stories show how children experience oppression and how it shapes their identity and resistance.

qa.q: The two accounts that you read above are based in two distant cultures. What is the commonality of theme found in both of them?
qa.a: Both accounts show the pain of oppression and discrimination. Zitkala-Sa suffers because of racial and cultural discrimination against Native Americans. Bama suffers because of caste discrimination. Both writers show how people from marginalised communities are humiliated and denied dignity.

qa.q: It may take a long time for oppression to be resisted, but the seeds of rebellion are sowed early in life. Do you agree that injustice in any form cannot escape being noticed even by children?
qa.a: Yes, children can notice injustice very clearly. Zitkala-Sa understood that cutting her hair was an insult to her culture, so she resisted by hiding under a bed. Bama first laughed at the way the elder carried the packet, but after her brother explained the caste reason, she felt sad, angry, and rebellious.

qa.q: Bama’s experience is that of a victim of the caste system. What kind of discrimination does Zitkala-Sa’s experience depict? What are their responses to their respective situations?
qa.a: Zitkala-Sa’s experience depicts racial and cultural discrimination. Her Native American identity was suppressed by forcing her to follow the white people’s ways, including cutting her long hair. She resisted strongly, but was overpowered. Bama faced caste discrimination and untouchability. Her response was anger and determination. After her brother advised her to study hard, she worked sincerely and stood first in class.
